Publication Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Energy Technologies (ICECET)
Abstract: The increasing popularity of the use of AI in everyday life has enabled the development of virtual systems for human assistance. This research proposes an implemented linear regression model with a vector of random weights, using a mean square error (MSE), based cost function and regularisation and corresponding Python code that integrates AI-based haptic feedback through vibration, specifically designed to optimise weight training.The results of the simulation model analysis show how key biomechanical parameters are identified and used to improve a person's posture by adjusting technique through real-time vibrational stimuli. The development of the system involves physical benefits such as injury prevention, continuous biomechanical monitoring, adaptive programme structuring and haptic vibration feedback for immediate movement correction. Popular and accurate tools for building linguistic models are mentioned, such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks. Technological tools such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks, applied to process biomechanical data and trigger personalised vibrational responses, are highlighted.

Document Title: Ownership Authentication and Clinical Data Backup in DICOM Images via Robust Watermarking and Binary Data Hiding
Authors: A. A. Lopez-Martinez; E. Fragoso-Navarro; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; F. Garcia-Ugalde
Author Affiliations: SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, CDMX, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 10th International Conference on Frontiers of Signal Processing (ICFSP)
Abstract: Medical information is very important and delicate due to its relevance in clinical diagnosis, treatments, research and other commercial and non-commercial applications of governmental and private organizations. Recent advances in information and communication technologies have transformed the paradigm of medical data management, but also introduced new risks related to unauthorized access, manipulation, and distribution. To solve part of these problems, this paper proposes a method that employs two information security levels based on robust watermarking and binary data hiding, respectively. Firstly, clinical data is embedded into a logo using binary data hiding. Then, this logo is inserted into the spatial domain of a medical image through block segmentation, statistical information, and convolutional encoding for ownership authentication. Experimental results demonstrate logo robustness against JPEG DICOM compression in conjunction with aggressive attacks like cropping and copy-move, using bit error rate as a metric. The imperceptibility has been measured via peak signal-to-noise ratio and structural similarity index metrics.
Author Keywords: Robust watermarking;Binary data hiding;DICOM imaging;Information security;Ownership authentication;Convolutional encoding
IEEE Terms: Measurement;Image coding;Bit error rate;Authentication;Transform coding;Information security;Watermarking;Robustness;DICOM;Medical diagnostic imaging

Document Title: AMIND: An Asynchronous Middleware for Improving Neighborhood Discovery in Crowded Networks
Authors: J. J. Camacho-Escoto; F. García; O. Arana-Hernández; J. Gomez
Author Affiliations: Department of Computing, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Telecommunications, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Telecommunications, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Telecommunications, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Access
Abstract: Neighbor discovery (ND) is crucial in deploying wireless sensor networks. Despite the considerable research on ND, most studies focus on networks composed of only two nodes. However, for cases in which more than two nodes are involved, the probability of collision increases, potentially preventing a node from discovering all its neighbors. This problem is especially aggravated for ND protocols that exhibit periodic behaviors that can lead to recurring collisions. This behavior is typical of deterministic ND protocols, in which its inherent operation forces regular on/off patterns and cyclical collisions. To address this problem, this paper introduces AMIND, a new middleware capable of disrupting the cyclic behavior of deterministic ND protocols. It achieves this goal by introducing random shifts in their operation, significantly increasing the number of nodes discovered compared to the existing ND protocols. As a result, AMIND allows a node to discover unknown neighbors after each shift. Simulation results show that the proposed middleware enhances node discoveries that previous ND protocols can achieve, even in scenarios with high collision rates.

IEEE Terms: Protocols;Middleware;Proposals;Schedules;Urban areas;Synchronization;Energy efficiency;Wireless sensor networks;Nearest neighbor methods

Document Title: Detection of DoS and DDoS Attacks Using LSTM in Computer Networks: A Systematic Literature Review
Authors: A. E. Godínez; J. L. Garza Rivera; D. T. Varela
Author Affiliations: CDMX, Universidad Da Vinci, México; CDMX, Universidad Da Vinci, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores Cuautitlán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Cuautitlan Izcalli, México
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Latin American School on Computational Intelligence and Robotics (SAIBOT)
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI), with machine learning (ML) models and deep learning (DL) neural networks, represents a possibility for detecting DoS/DDoS attacks more effectively. The LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) model is one of the options for detecting these increasingly varied and sophisticated attacks. The objective of this research was to evaluate the application of the LSTM model in the detection of DoS and DDoS cyber attacks on communications networks. This research was designed as a Systematic Literature Review to obtain the most recent studies on the subject. As a result, 86 initial studies were obtained from the databases. Once the entire procedure, including the quality assessment of the studies, was completed, 15 final primary studies were selected. With these primary studies, it was possible to answer all the questions in the review. It should be noted that modern SDN (Software Defined Network) networks, Cloud Computing networks, and classic networks are threatened by this type of attack. The LSTM models applied in the detection of DoS/DDoS attacks can be hybrid or combined. The most commonly used evaluation metrics are Accuracy, Recall, Precision, and F1-Score, and the most frequently found public datasets include NSL-KDD, CICDoS2019, and CICIDS2017. A wide variety of LSTM-based models and approaches that can be used to experiment and compare their performance in terms of communications network security.
Author Keywords: Long short term memory;Systematic literature review;Machine learning;Denial-of-service attack;Network security

Authors: A. Hernández-Benítez; J. Vázquez-Castillo; J. J. Estrada-López; G. Becerra-Nunez; N. Cólin-García; A. Castillo-Atoche
Author Affiliations: Faculty of Engineering, Autonomous University of Yucatan, Mérida, Mexico; Engineering Department, Autonomous University of Quintana Roo, Chetumal, Mexico; Faculty of Mathematics, Autonomous University of Yucatan, Mérida, Mexico; Engineering Department, Autonomous University of Quintana Roo, Chetumal, Mexico; Molecular and Cell Biology Lab, National School of Higher Studies, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Faculty of Engineering, Autonomous University of Yucatan, Mérida, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Embedded Systems Letters
Abstract: Hydroponic farming is a promising alternative to soil-based farming. However, it requires a precise control of the growth environment, which is hard to achieve with energy-constrained embedded systems. This letter presents an energy optimization technique for the continuous operation of energy harvesting-based hydroponics sensor nodes. The proposed technique is based on the self-tuning model, that dynamically adjust the duty cycle of the node, ensuring the autonomous operation of the Internet of Things system. The model can be programmed in a low-power microcontroller, allowing the decision-making process to reside entirely on the sensor node. Experimental results show that in the same time period, the self-tuning model allows  $3.5\times $  more data transmissions than a uniform 5-min duty cycle, while ensuring a minimum voltage level in the storage device. This balance allows the stored energy to be enough for continuous monitoring, providing a clean and cost-effective alternative to perpetually power the hydroponic system.

Abstract: In this paper, we conduct an energy consumption analysis of a broadcast access mechanism over IEEE 802.11 networks. By assuming non-saturated conditions, we provide a statistical model of the energy consumed during one cycle period, defined as the duration between two successful broadcast transmissions. Our results show that the broadcast protocol is a good candidate to be included in future amendments of the IEEE 802.11 standard.

Document Title: ODIF: Optimum Duty Cycle Selection Using Indirect Feedback for Neighborhood-Discovery in Crowded Scenarios
Authors: J. J. Camacho-Escoto; J. Gomez; L. Orozco-Barbosa
Author Affiliations: Department of Computer Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Telecommunications Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Albacete Research Institute of Informatics, Universidad de Castilla-La Mancha, Albacete, Spain
Publication Title: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
Abstract: A significant amount of research has focused on minimizing the time it takes for two nodes to discover each other when operating in on-off states to conserve energy, a process known as neighbor discovery. However, less attention has been paid to the related issue of neighborhood-discovery, which involves multiple nodes attempting to find each other. We consider the latter problem to be quite relevant, especially with the increasing popularity of networks made up of multiple IoT devices. This issue is more complex because the likelihood of collisions increases with a greater number of nodes and as the time nodes remain awake (duty cycle) extends. The method we propose, called ODIF, uses indirect feedback (collisions and no-encounters) to estimate the number of nodes and employs a dynamic duty cycle algorithm to minimize either latency or energy expenditure during neighborhood-discovery. We compare ODIF with a randomized discovery protocol (referred to as Plain) and two other similar methods found in the existing literature. It was found that ODIF significantly reduces the neighbor discovery latency (or the expected number of attempts) compared to the baseline randomized neighbor discovery protocol for most values of the duty cycle. Similarly, ODIF achieved neighborhood-discovery 1 to 10 times faster for high node densities compared to similar protocols.

Publication Title: 2024 International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE)
Abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) devices generate vast and often contradictory data streams. This poses a challenge for IoT systems, which must make accurate and reliable decisions based on this data. Paraconsistent descriptive logic (PDL) is a branch of non-classical logic that can handle inconsistencies without compromising data reliability. We propose the integration of PDL into IoT systems to improve data coherence and reliability. We also introduce a Consistency Theorem that establishes rules for managing inconsistencies systematically and in a controlled way. Our efforts aim to pave the way for more robust, reliable, and user-friendly IoT systems.

Communication Engineering, Bilbao, Spain
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
Abstract: The fields of human presence detection (HPD) and people counting (PC) from WiFi-Channel State Information (CSI) represent constantly evolving areas of research within the scientific community. This study aims to expand the research frontiers by exploring the application of HPD and PC in an industrial environment characterized by its considerable dimensions, the presence of obstacles and machines, and harsh propagation channel conditions. Using a convolutional neural network (CNN) incorporating CSI measurements, we demonstrated the ability to distinguish between human presence and industrial machine activity with the same model, achieving an accuracy of over 99%. In addition, a study was conducted to count up to three people, even when machines were on, with an accuracy of over 92%. Another experiment was conducted with the objective of assessing not only the feasibility of PC in a naturally challenging environment but also the impact of the WiFi channel bandwidth in this particular application. The results of this study highlight the potential of WiFi-CSI sensing to address a significant challenge in human activity detection and estimation: its application in industrial environments.

Abstract: This article presents the integration of advanced artificial intelligence algorithms applied to collaborative robots (cobots) in five strategic sectors: underground mining, geriatric medicine, precision agriculture, port logistics, and highprecision manufacturing. It proposes the use of Graph Convolutional Neural Networks (Graph-CNNs) to model spatiotemporal motion; Multimodal Transformers to fuse heterogeneous sensor data; Soft Actor-Critic with Constrained Policy Optimization (SAC-RCPO) for safe and adaptive control; and TactileGAN to generate realistic haptic feedback. Within this framework, multimodal AI improves operational efficiency, task accuracy, and the quality of human-robot interaction. In practice, it contributes to risk reduction in hazardous environments such as mining and logistics, improves accuracy in agricultural and manufacturing tasks, and fosters greater acceptance among older adults in healthcare settings. A review of existing literature and simulations in agriculture and mining confirms performance improvements and supports the feasibility of integrating algorithms into collaborative robotic systems.

Publication Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Energy Technologies (ICECET)
Abstract: In today’s digital landscape, the exchange of sensitive personal data via mobile devices is widespread, yet inadequate safeguards often expose users to risks such as identity theft and online harassment. This research introduces Kamplae, a system crafted in Python and deployed through a Flask-based REST API, employing homomorphic encryption and artificial intelligence to protect personal information embedded in digital images. Kamplae secures metadata and bitmap content in jpeg, jpg, and png formats, adhering to legal standards in Mexico and the European Union.The study reviews prior work on personal data protection and the challenges posed by deep fakes, emphasising the need for robust regulatory frameworks to counter digital violence. It explores various encryption methods—symmetric, asymmetric, and hybrid—alongside relevant legal provisions across regions.Kamplae aims to prevent image misuse and foster a secure, ethical digital environment. While promising, it highlights areas for enhancement, such as broader format support and user interface refinement, with limitations tied to specific format compatibility and mobile performance.

Document Title: Symmetric Cipher for Grayscale Images Based on Quantum Differential Geometry
Authors: E. Gonzalez-Ramos; L. Olvera-Martinez; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; C. Diaz-Rodriguez; L. Faustino-Morales
Author Affiliations: Instituto Politecnico Nacional, SEPI ESIME Culhuacan Ciudad de Mexico, Mexico; Instituto Politecnico Nacional, SEPI ESIME Culhuacan Ciudad de Mexico, Mexico; Instituto Politecnico Nacional, SEPI ESIME Culhuacan Ciudad de Mexico, Mexico; Instituto Politecnico Nacional, SEPI ESIME Culhuacan Ciudad de Mexico, Mexico; Facultad de Ciencias Ciudad de Mexico, Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 21st International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Computing Science and Automatic Control (CCE)
Abstract: The increase in information technologies has generated the need to implement new methodologies to secure the exchange of information. This paper proposes a cryptographic algorithm based on the use of quantum differential geometry operators. These operators exhibit mathematical behaviors similar to quantum behavior, with the advantage that specialized equipment is not required to perform the encryption process. The algorithm is composed of two phases: In the first phase, a subkey is generated through an addition and multiplication process in Z_16 based on a user-defined key. In the second phase, three types of fundamental quantum operators are employed: creation, annihilation, and crossing, which are sequentially connected. The specific selection and configuration of these operators are based on their ability to avoid generating correlation between the data. The method proposed in this work is implemented using grayscale images, obtaining the Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM), and the Correlation Value, in order to evaluate the fidelity and integrity of the recovered images.
Author Keywords: Subkey Generation;Quantum Operators;Grayscale Images;Configurable Cipher;Finite Fields
IEEE Terms: Geometry;Ciphers;Visualization;PSNR;Correlation;Quantum computing;Merging;Gray-scale;Encryption;Proposals

Document Title: Color Image Protection via Quantum Encryption and HVS-Based Mini-Block Data Hiding
Authors: L. Olvera-Martinez; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; C. Diaz-Rodriguez; L. Faustinos-Morales
Author Affiliations: Instituto Politécnico Nacional Escuela Superior de Ingeniería, Mecánica y Eléctrica Unidad Culhuacan, CDMX, México; Instituto Politécnico Nacional Escuela Superior de Ingeniería, Mecánica y Eléctrica Unidad Culhuacan, CDMX, México; Instituto Politécnico Nacional Escuela Superior de Ingeniería, Mecánica y Eléctrica Unidad Culhuacan, CDMX, México; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de Mexico, CDMX, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 10th International Conference on Frontiers of Signal Processing (ICFSP)
Abstract: With the growing demand for secure and efficient digital image processing, the integration of data hiding and encryption techniques has become essential to ensure both confidentiality and integrity of visual information. This paper proposes a hybrid approach that combines a low complexity steganographic scheme for binary and color images with a computationally efficient encryption technique inspired by quantum operators. In the data hiding component, an RGB channel of a color image is selected, and its least significant bit plane is extracted. This bit plane is then divided into suitable blocks, and HVS-based encoding tables are used to embed the information within these blocks, achieving high embedding capacity while preserving visual quality. In parallel, the encryption stage operates on color images and is based on a novel operator model that simulates quantum behaviors such as creation, exchange, and annihilation, enabling effective protection without requiring intensive mathematical processing. Experimental validation confirms the robustness of the combined approach through quality metrics such as PSNR, SSIM and entropy.
Author Keywords: Braided Quantum;Quantum Operators;Dynamic Matrices;Finite Fields
IEEE Terms: Visualization;Steganography;Signal processing algorithms;Color;Signal processing;Robustness;Real-time systems;Encryption;Data mining;Protection

Document Title: Tensorization of $f$-Divergences
Authors: R. Cruz; M. Diaz; F. P. Calmon
Author Affiliations: Harvard University, Cambridge, MA, USA; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Harvard University, Cambridge, MA, USA
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory (ISIT)
Abstract: In many applications across statistics, differential privacy, and machine learning, it is necessary to evaluate or bound an $f$-divergence between distributions of random vectors with independent components. Even for relatively simple distributions, such computations can become intractable unless the chosen $f$-divergence tensorizes. In this work, we introduce a formal definition of tensorization for $f$-divergences and present a necessary condition under which such tensorization can occur. Moreover, we demonstrate—under certain assumptions—that the only $f$-divergences admitting a polynomial tensorization formula of degree at most two are, essentially, the KL divergence, cross-entropy, and Hellinger divergences of order $\alpha$. Taken together, our findings represent an initial step toward a complete characterization of $f$-divergences that tensorize.
Author Keywords: f-divergence;tensorization
IEEE Terms: Differential privacy;Additives;Total variance;Machine learning;Polynomials;Vectors;Information theory

Document Title: Enhancing the Capacity and Robustness of an LSB Algorithm Using a Novel Insertion Method, Hashing Function, and Secret Key
Authors: A. I. H. Al-Jarah; J. L. Ortega-Arjona
Author Affiliations: Postgraduate in Science and Computing Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Mathematics Department, Science Faculty, National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Access
Abstract: Steganography hides a secret message inside another medium (cover-image) while maintaining imperceptibility and/or robustness/embedding capacity, making it a suitable candidate for keeping information safe and hiding the existence of data itself inside another medium. Secret data is susceptible to attacks or may be modified while steg-image is transiting through the Internet. The present paper explains how secret key steganography uses a new insertion method to hide information inside an image and a hashing function applied on the secret data. The SHA 256 function detects errors in data and assures its arrival without modifications. The proposed algorithm improves the security level by adding a robustness parameter (secret key and hash function) and creating a new insertion method that doubles the cover-image capacity to hide a secret message inside a color image compared with LSB, useful for keeping sensitive, relevant, and important information invulnerable. The results are compared with the LSB algorithm and related work in terms of the peak signal to noise ratio (PSNR) values, cover-image maximum capacity (MX), image quality index (Q), bit per pixel (BPP), processing time and robustness, showing that the proposed algorithm has a great balance between capacity and PSNR (big capacity with high PSNR level). It also increases the robustness level using secret key and hash function without affecting image imperceptibility. The proposed algorithm speed is outstanding, despite having complex calculations. This makes it eligible to be used in online platforms.

Document Title: $\mathrm{E}_{\gamma}$-Mixing Time
Authors: B. Zamanlooy; S. Asoodeh; M. Diaz; F. P. Calmon
Author Affiliations: Department of Computing and Software, McMaster University, Canada; Department of Computing and Software, McMaster University, Canada; IIMAS, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico; School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, USA
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory (ISIT)
Abstract: We investigate the mixing times of Markov kernels under $\mathsf{E}_{\gamma}-\mathbf{divergence}$. We demonstrate that the zero-error $\mathsf{E}_{\gamma}- \mathbf{mixing}$ time, for any $\gamma > 1$, of irreducible and aperiodic Markov chains, is bounded, a property that is not shared by the TV-mixing time. We further obtain upper bounds on the $\mathsf{E}_{\gamma}-\mathbf{mixing}$ times for a broad family of contractive Markov kernels via a new non-linear strong data processing inequality for the $\mathsf{E}_{\gamma}- \mathbf{divergence}$. We apply our results to derive new bounds for the local differential privacy guarantees offered by the sequential application of a privacy mechanism to data.

Document Title: On the Privacy Guarantees of Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent
Authors: S. Asoodeh; M. Diaz
Author Affiliations: Department of Computing and Software, McMaster University, Canada; IIMAS, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory (ISIT)
Abstract: Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD) is a widely adopted algorithm for privately training machine learning models. An inherent feature of this algorithm is the incorporation of gradient clipping to counteract the influence of individual samples during training. Nevertheless, the introduction of gradient clipping also introduces non-convexity into the problem, rendering it challenging to derive upper bounds on the privacy loss. In this paper, we establish effective upper bounds for the privacy loss of both projected DP-SGD and regularized DP-SGD, without relying on convexity or smoothness assumptions regarding the loss function. Our approach involves a direct analysis of the hockey-stick divergence between coupled stochastic processes through the application of nonlinear data processing inequalities.

Document Title: Invited Paper: Further Decentralizing Decentralized Finance: Loss vs Rebalancing Without a Shared Reference Market
Authors: M. Herlihy; S. Rajsbaum
Author Affiliations: Computer Science Department, Brown University, Providence, USA; Instituto de Matemáticas UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 6th Conference on Blockchain Research & Applications for Innovative Networks and Services (BRAINS)
Abstract: Automated market makers (AMMs) typically rely on arbitrage agents to keep prices in line with a shared reference market such as a large centralized exchange. This paper considers an alternative, even-more-decentralized model where prices must stabilize without a shared reference market.We first consider a model where there is one population of AMMs, and another of arbitrage agents who seek to profit from pairwise price differences between randomly-chosen AMMs. For constant-product AMMs, repeated random pair-wise arbitrage causes the AMMs’ expected prices to converge within any precision ϵ > 0 in $\Theta \left( {\max \left( {{n^2}\log n,\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon }} \right)} \right.$ interactions, and arbitrage agents’ profits are proportional to the original price imbalances. Within certain limits, the arbitrage agents can collude to set the final stable price.If, instead, randomly-chosen pairs of AMMs could rebalance their asset pools directly, capturing profits that would have gone to arbitrage agents, then expected AMM prices converge within ϵ with respective upper and lower bounds of $\Omega \left( {\max \left( {{n^2}\log n,\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon }} \right)} \right.$ and $O\left( {\max \left( {{n^2}\log n,\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon }} \right)} \right.$ interactions. Within certain limits, the AMMs can collude to set the final stable price.

Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
Abstract: This article introduces a novel approach to addressing supply chain and logistics challenges using ramified optimal transport. This framework provides a flexible and efficient means of modeling nonlinear and realistic scenarios within natural and complex transportation networks, particularly in real-time applications where route optimization is critical. The problem considered involves determining optimal warehouse locations and devising distribution strategies for delivering goods from suppliers to customers. To this end, we developed two new estimation algorithms and benchmarked them against traditional and heuristic methods. The proposed algorithms demonstrated competitive performance in solving supply chain and logistics problems, with one of them achieving the best results in terms of computational cost. Finally, the algorithms were applied to real-world data from the cement industry, successfully producing real-time optimal routes. This was made possible by the incorporation of a carefully designed distance function in the cost modeling.

Author Affiliations: ITSE, FESC-UNAM, Cuautitlán Izcalli, México; Electrónica, FESC-UNAM, Cuautitlán Izcalli, México; Eléctrica, FESC-UNAM, Cuautitlán Izcalli, México; Electrónica, FESC-UNAM, Cuautitlán Izcalli, México
Publication Title: 2025 XXVII Robotics Mexican Congress (COMRob)
Abstract: Human-robot interaction is a core pillar in mobile robotics for efficient and diverse product development. This paper provides an advanced web-based control interface for a differential robot which allows full management and real-time monitoring of its behavior. It operates through an ESP32 microcontroller where both sides communicate with a web client using the WebSocket protocol. There are three operating modes: manual by sliders, autonomous navigation toward distinct (X, Y) coordinates, and a pioneering trajectory-tracking mode that lets the operator generate a path directly on a digital canvas. Also, the user interface has a monitoring panel with real-time information on such things as motor RPM, position, and robot orientation, all supported by dynamic charts. Differential kinematics and sensor fusion enable hardware and software integration by merging encoder-based odometry with an IMU for more precise positioning. The final outcome is a powerful, intuitive, and flexible platform which serves as a solid basis for future applications in autonomous exploration, logistics, and educational environments.

Authors: D. Haro-Mendoza; G. A. Castro Vazquez; A. Leonardo Campos Carreras; A. J. Diaz Urias; V. J. Gonzalez-Villela; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; R. E. Arevalo-Ancona
Author Affiliations: Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Biomedical Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Communications, Information, Electronic and Energy Systems (CIEES)
Abstract: Surgical procedures can be decomposed into elementary units (tasks, surgemes, and dexemes) that represent the building blocks of surgical performance. Segmenting gestures at this level is crucial for objective skill assessment and for understanding how simple movements are concatenated to form complete procedures, ultimately supporting the development of training and evaluation systems. In this work, we present a pseudo-temporal modeling approach for surgical gesture segmentation using a 2D ResNet-50 convolutional network trained exclusively on RGB frames. During training, each video was divided into eight equal segments, which were used to feed the network and generate gesture-level probabilities. To optimize learning, an adaptive Exponential Moving Average (EMA) mechanism was applied to stabilize weight updates and reinforce poorly performing gesture classes. At inference, complete surgical videos were processed with an overlapping sliding window of eight frames, where each frame could be evaluated multiple times in different contexts. Frame-level probabilities were averaged across overlapping windows and refined through a temporal mode filter, producing stable and coherent gesture sequences. The method was validated on the JIGSAWS dataset, which includes the Suturing, Knot Tying, and Needle Passing tasks. Results showed reliable recognition across all procedures: Suturing achieved 82.26%, Knot Tying 91.97%, and Needle Passing 79.35%. Training required approximately one hour, and inference on a complete video of 2950 frames was performed in about 45 seconds, also generating a labeled output video. By relying solely on 2D convolutions and RGB input, the approach achieves accurate and efficient segmentation with low computational cost, making it suitable for surgical training environments with limited resources and applicable in near real-time scenarios.

Document Title: A Reasoning System for Graded Modal Logic
Authors: R. López-Villafán; E. Bárcenas
Author Affiliations: IIMAS: Posgrado en Ciencia e lngeniería de la Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingenería: Dpto. de Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 12th International Conference in Software Engineering Research and Innovation (CONISOFT)
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a deduction system for the modal logic $\mathbf{K}$ with graded modalities, which is obtained by adding numerical constraints to the modal operators. Graded modalities can be seen as a generalization of traditional modal operators since they include numerical bounds in the modalities, thus enabling the formal treatment of more detailed expressions, such as “there are at least $n+1$ individuals satisfying a certain property”, where $n$ is a natural number. This kind of properties commonly arise in contexts such as concurrent systems: “if at least 3 processors are free, then proceed to compute the next task”, or epistemic logic:”if more than 2 people know $P$, then $P$ is known”. The system is based on the notion of tree-hypersequents. We show the system is correct, i.e. sound and complete.
Author Keywords: graded modalities;proof theory;hypersequents;automated reasoning

Abstract: The first steps towards integrating autonomous monitoring, probabilistic forecasting, reachability analysis, and adaptive sampling for the Gulf of Mexico were demonstrated in real-time during the collaborative Mini-Adaptive Sampling Test Run (MASTR) ocean experiment, which took place from February to April 2024. The emphasis of this contribution is on the use of the MIT Multidisciplinary Simulation, Estimation, and Assimilation Systems (MSEAS) including Error Subspace Statistical Estimation (ESSE) large-ensemble forecasting and path planning systems to predict ocean fields and uncertainties, forecast reachable sets and optimal paths for gliders, and guide sampling aircraft and ocean vehicles toward the most informative observations. Deterministic and probabilistic ocean forecasts are exemplified and linked to the variability of the Loop Current (LC) and LC Eddies, demonstrating predictive skill by real-time comparisons to independent data. Risk forecasts in terms of probabilities of currents exceeding 1.5 kt were provided. The most informative sampling patterns for Remote Ocean Current Imaging System (ROCIS) flights were forecast using mutual information between surface currents and density anomaly. Finally, we guided four underwater gliders using probabilistic reachability and path-planning forecasts.

Abstract: As part of the Mini-Adaptive Sampling Test Run (MASTR) experiment in the Gulf of Mexico (GoM) region from February to April 2024, we demonstrated real-time deterministic and probabilistic reachability analysis and time-optimal path planning to guide a fleet of four ocean gliders. The governing differential equations for reachability analysis and time-optimal path planning were numerically integrated in real-time and forced by currents from our large-ensemble ocean forecasts. We illustrate the real-time deterministic and probabilistic forward reachability analyses, reachability and path planning for glider pickups, time-optimal path planning for gliders in distress, and planning of future glider deployments. Results show that the actual paths of gliders were contained within our reachable set forecasts and in accord with the dynamic reachability fronts. Our time-optimal headings and paths also predicted real glider motions, even for longer-range predictions of weeks to a month duration. Reachability and time-optimal path planning forecasts were successfully employed for glider recovery. They also enabled exploring options for future glider deployments.

Document Title: A Parallel Steganography Algorithm Using a New Secret Key Generation
Authors: A. I. H. Al-Jarah; J. L. O. Arjona
Author Affiliations: Postgraduate in Science and Computing Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Mathematics department, Science faculty National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE 15th Annual Ubiquitous Computing, Electronics & Mobile Communication Conference (UEMCON)
Abstract: Computation has mostly used parallelism for high-performance computation, and multicore processors have gained popularity because they offer better processing time. However, one of the most challenging tasks to achieve is securely exchange confidential data over the network between sender and receiver. The research work in this paper explains how secret key steganography, combined with parallel processing, is utilized to conceal secret data within an image. The proposed algorithm describes and focuses on the generation method of a secret key while merging it with the Least Significant Bit (LSB) algorithm to achieve robustness, as well as parallel computing to improve the processing time in both the encoding/decoding phases. The proposed algorithm adds another layer of security by adding a robustness parameter (secret key), convenient for keeping secret, confidential, and crucial data. Parallel processing, combined with the new method to formulate the secret key is the novelty and strongest attribute of the proposed algorithm. When parallel computing is applied, the process speeds up compared with the same algorithm in the sequential version. The results are compared with the LSB algorithm in terms of the peak signal to noise ratio (PSNR) and mean square error (MSE) values, aspiring to present that the proposed algorithm adds robustness without affecting image imperceptibility.
Author Keywords: Hiding data;Image steganography;Processing time improvement;Parallel steganography;Secret key steganography;Steganography
IEEE Terms: Steganography;Visualization;PSNR;Program processors;Multicore processing;Receivers;Parallel processing;Mobile communication;Robustness;Security

Document Title: Data-Driven Power System Linear Model Identification via Quadrature-Based Balanced Truncation
Authors: M. R. Arrieta Paternina; J. A. Moreno Corbea; J. M. Ramirez-Arredondo; J. H. Chow; A. Zamora-Mendez
Author Affiliations: Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (UNAM), MX; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (UNAM), MX; Centro de Investigacion y de Estudios Avanzados del IPN, Unidad Guadalajara, MX; Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, US; Universidad Michoacana San Nicolas de Hidalgo, MX
Publication Title: IEEE Latin America Transactions
Abstract: This paper introduces the novel formulation of the quadrature-based balanced truncation (QBBT) method to precisely identify large-scale power system linear models. The QBBT interpolates a directional frequency dataset that is extracted from a dynamic system, following a quadrature rule to be applied from weighted data to derive a linear system model based on approximations of Gramians, and providing an alternative approach to the classic balanced truncation (BT) formulation that makes use of the system model. In this investigation, an accurate theoretical framework is presented to achieve the application of QBBT considering power systems as black-box models. The Boyd/Clenshaw-Curtis (BCC) quadrature rule is implemented by using different adjustments to accurately identify linear models of different order. The attained results and their validation with analytical power system models confirm the method's potential to retail oscillatory dynamics. The QBBT's effectiveness is compared with the Loewner-based frequency interpolation (LBFI) approach and the BT method on the Klein-Rogers-Kundur (KRK) benchmark power system and the equivalent of the New England transmission system - New York power system (NETS-NYPS), achieving a small absolute error in the magnitude of the frequency response of the models of the order of 1x10-3 dB in comparison with other state-of-the-art methods.
Author Keywords: balanced truncation;quadrature rules;system identification;directional frequency data-set;power system linear model

Document Title: Detection of Optimal Echocardiographic Video Segments Using Deep Learning
Authors: I. Perez-Ruiz; J. Perez-Gonzalez; A. Carrera-Pinzón; R. E.; R. H.; B. Y.; A. C. F.
Author Affiliations: Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM; Universidad Nacional de Colombia; Universidad Nacional de Colombia; Hospital Universitario Nacional de Colombia; Hospital Universitario Nacional de Colombia; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM
Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: The identification of optimal echocardiographic video segments is a critical initial step for reliable cardiac function assessment. This study focuses on the development and evaluation of deep learning models for the automated classification of correct versus incorrect echocardiographic planes in a video sequence, specifically for Subcostal Four-Chamber (S4C) views. A dataset comprising 10 S4C studies was used, from which frames were extracted and preprocessed. Preprocessing included metadata removal, resizing to 224×224 pixels, and data augmentation. The resulting images were divided into training (2068 images per class), validation (517 images per class), and test (200 images per class) sets. Two convolutional neural network architectures, VGG-16 and Vision Transformer (ViT), were trained and evaluated. The VGG-16 model, trained for 30 epochs, achieved a test accuracy of 0.98 and an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 1.00. The Vision Transformer, trained for 30 epochs, yielded a test accuracy of 0.89 and an AUC of 0.97. These preliminary results demonstrate the potential of deep learning models, particularly VGG-16, to accurately identify optimal frames in echocardiographic video sequences.
IEEE Terms: Deep learning;Training;Computer vision;Accuracy;Video sequences;Metadata;Transformers;Data augmentation;Reliability;Videos
